text: Theories of intraparty competition often rely on the assumption of given electoral circumstances and party rules. This article contributes to the black box by studying intraparty preferences. We deduce and test two hypotheses: that intraparty preference heterogeneity is higher after party failures than successes, and that candidates who are far away from the party median tend to collect fewer intra-party votes than those closer to the median. We hypothesise that intra-party members trust more freely a candidate whose role in the party is strengthened by being in government, and allows for greater scope to balance out demands for competing policy issues. We use speeches from the party congresses of political parties from Germany and France to empirically investigate the relationship between voting outcomes and intra-party preferences and to examine more generally the character of intra-party politics. We develop a new dataset based on a large-scale automated coding exercise of speech acts from party congresses, and using text classification to infer policy preferences for the competing speakers. Speeches at party conferences are shown to provide unique and valuable information for the study of politicians’ preferences, intra-party politics and voting behaviour. The study also explores the relationship between policy preferences of political parties in power and the state of the economy, and the complex relationship between the governmental context of parties, the economic situation and intra-party disagreement.
